nativeRange.habitat: Clear, well-oxygenated streams, small pools, and rivers with dense vegetation in western central Sumatra; type locality is Lake Maninjau in West Sumatra.

diet.notes: Readily accepts a varied diet in the aquarium. Offer quality dried flakes or micro-pellets alongside frozen or live foods such as bloodworm, daphnia, artemia, and tubifex. Wild populations have been documented feeding on aquatic invertebrates, diatoms, insects, crustaceans, worms, and smaller amounts of plant material and algae.

avoidWith: very small nano fish that may be outcompeted

summary: A peaceful, beginner-friendly schooling barb from Sumatra, known for its iridescent checkered scale pattern. Generally considered one of the calmer barb species and less likely to nip fins than Tiger Barbs; best kept in groups of 6 or more.
careNotes: The Checker Barb was reclassified from Puntius (and before that Capoeta) into the genus Oliotius in 2013, though older trade and hobby literature still commonly uses Puntius oligolepis — a name that appears on many retailer labels today. The species is endemic to western central Sumatra, Indonesia, with its type locality at Lake Maninjau. It is commercially farmed, and wild-caught specimens are rarely available in the trade. Males tend to be slimmer and more colourful than females, displaying reddish-brown dorsal, pelvic, and anal fins distinctly outlined in black; females are typically rounder with plainer yellowish-clear fins. The iridescent checkered scale pattern — each scale appearing half black and half silver — is particularly striking under aquarium lighting. Compared to the Tiger Barb, the Checker Barb is generally regarded as considerably more peaceful, with little tendency toward fin-nipping when kept in a proper school. A group of 6–10 individuals in a planted aquarium with open swimming areas and subdued or dappled lighting tends to produce the best natural behaviour and colouration.
breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer with no parental care. Spawning has been triggered by conditioning pairs or trios on live and frozen foods for several weeks, sometimes combined with a simulated temperature drop. A dedicated breeding tank with fine-leaved plants or spawning mops over a mesh bottom is advised to protect eggs from egg-eating adults. Eggs generally hatch within 24–48 hours; fry typically become free-swimming within another 24 hours and are generally reported to reach adulthood within 4–6 months. Breeding difficulty is broadly rated as moderate due to the fry-rearing demands, though triggering spawning is not considered especially difficult.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Primary source (swapped from Aquarium Co-Op, which returned 404 — no dedicated checker barb care page found). Provided taxonomy, native range, tank dimensions (75×30×30 cm), water parameters (18–25°C, pH 5.5–7.5, 36–215 ppm hardness), adult size 40–45 mm, diet, and breeding notes.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name (Oliotius oligolepis, Bleeker 1853), taxonomy (Cyprinidae / Cypriniformes / Smiliogastrinae), size (5.0 cm TL), water parameters (20–24°C, pH 6.0–6.5, up to 10 dH), native range (Sumatra, Indonesia). Confirmed IUCN Least Concern status.

additional.name: Aquadiction
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (5 cm), temperature (20–23°C), pH (5.5–6.5), gH (1–10), lifespan (up to 8 years), schooling requirement (6–10 individuals), diet, decor preferences, and breeding method. Temperature and pH ranges narrower than SeriouslyFish; averaged across sources. Lifespan upper end of 8 years noted though 4–6 is the broader consensus.

fish.breedingNotes: The Checker Barb is an egg-scatterer that spawns among fine-leaved plants or over a spawning mop. Adults will eat their own eggs, so parents should be removed from the breeding tank after spawning. Eggs typically hatch within 24–48 hours, and fry are usually free-swimming within another day.
